source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A beam of neutrons passes through a hydrogen target (density $4 \times$ $10^{22}$ atom $/ \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ ) and is detected in a counter C as shown in Fig. 4.10. For equal incident beam flux, $5.0 \times 10^{5}$ counts are recorded in C with the target empty, and $4.6 \times 10^{5}$ with the target full of hydrogen. Estimate the total $n-p$ scattering cross section, and its statistical error.

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A beam of neutrons passes through a hydrogen target (density $4 \times$ $10^{22}$ atom $/ \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ ) and is detected in a counter C as shown in Fig. 4.10. For equal incident beam flux, $5.0 \times 10^{5}$ counts are recorded in C with the target empty, and $4.6 \times 10^{5}$ with the target full of hydrogen. Estimate the total $n-p$ scattering cross section, and its statistical error.
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image object string representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain:
Let the total cross section of $n-p$ interaction be $\sigma$. After passing through the hydrogen target, the number of neutrons decreases from $N_{0}$ to $N_{0} e^{-n \sigma t}$,
where $n=4 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ is the atomic concentration of the target. Suppose the numbers of neutrons detected without and with the hydrogen target are $N^{\prime}, N^{\prime \prime}$ respectively and $\eta$ is the neutron-detecting efficiency of C. Then
$$
N^{\prime}=\eta N_{0}, \quad N^{\prime \prime}=\eta N_{0} e^{-n \sigma t}=N^{\prime} e^{-n \sigma t}
$$
and thus
$$
N^{\prime \prime} / N^{\prime}=e^{-n \sigma t}
$$
giving the $n-p$ scattering cross section as
$$
\sigma=\frac{1}{n t} \ln \frac{N^{\prime}}{N^{\prime \prime}}=\frac{1}{4 \times 10^{22} \times 100} \ln \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{4.6 \times 10^{5}}=2.08 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} .
$$

To estimate the statistical error of $\sigma$ we note
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \sigma & =\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial N^{\prime}}\left(\Delta N^{\prime}\right)+\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial N^{\prime}}\left(\Delta N^{\prime}\right) 
\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial N^{\prime}} & =\frac{1}{n t N^{\prime}}, 
\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial N^{\prime \prime}} & =-\frac{1}{n t N^{\prime \prime}}, 
\Delta N^{\prime} & =\sqrt{N^{\prime}}, \quad \Delta N^{\prime \prime}=\sqrt{N^{\prime \prime}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence
$$
(\Delta \sigma)^{2}=\left(\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial N^{\prime}}\right)^{2}\left(\Delta N^{\prime}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial N^{\prime \prime}}\right)^{2}\left(\Delta N^{\prime \prime}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{(n t)^{2}}\left(\frac{1}{N^{\prime}}+\frac{1}{N^{\prime \prime}}\right)
$$or
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \sigma & =\frac{1}{(n t)} \sqrt{\frac{1}{N^{\prime}}+\frac{1}{N^{\prime \prime}}}=\frac{1}{4 \times 10^{22} \times 100} \sqrt{\frac{1}{4.6 \times 10^{5}}+\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{5}}} 
& \approx 5 \times 10^{-28} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore
$$
\sigma=(2.08 \pm 0.05) \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}=(20.8 \pm 0.5) \mathrm{mb}
$$
